population: Patients with sepsis admitted to ICU (MIMIC-IV database)
cohort_age: Non-survivors: median 70 years; Survivors: median 66 years
notes: U-shaped association between HCT and 30-day mortality; HCT 35-<40% used as reference group; lowest mortality risk at HCT 35-40%

population: Patients with sepsis admitted to ICU (MIMIC-IV database)
cohort_age: Non-survivors: median 70 years; Survivors: median 66 years
performance: Non-linear relationship confirmed, p for non-linearity <0.05
notes: RCS demonstrated a U-shaped non-linear relationship between HCT and 30-day mortality in sepsis patients

source_info.section: RESEARCH ARTICLE

population: Patients with sepsis admitted to ICU (MIMIC-IV database)

source_info.section: RESEARCH ARTICLE

population: Patients with sepsis admitted to ICU, subgroup analysis by SOFA score
cohort_age: Non-survivors: median 70 years; Survivors: median 66 years
effect_size: Consistent U-shaped association across subgroups stratified by age, gender, SOFA score, and comorbidities
performance: No significant interaction detected across most subgroups (p for interaction >0.05)
notes: Subgroup analyses confirmed robustness of the U-shaped association; results were consistent across different patient subgroups

source_info.section: OPEN ACCESS

population: Septic patients hospitalized in an advanced academic medical center in Boston, Massachusetts (MIMIC-IV database)
cohort_age: Non-survivors: median 70 years; Survivors: median 66 years
method: Multivariate Cox proportional hazards regression model
performance: p<0.05; 30-day mortality rates: low HCT 62.6%, regular HCT 27.5%, high HCT 9.9%
notes: Low HCT identified as independent risk factor for 30-day mortality in sepsis patients. Study period 2008–2019. HCT classified into three groups: low, regular, and high.

source_info.section: Abstract

population: Sepsis 3.0 patients admitted to ICU (MIMIC-IV database)
cohort_age: Non-survivors: median 70 years; Survivors: median 66 years
method: Comparison between survival and death groups
notes: Death group had lower hematocrit levels compared to survival group (P<0.05). 911 deaths within 30 days. Death group was also older with lower albumin, platelets, and hemoglobin levels.

source_info.section: **Population and baseline characteristics**

cohort_age: Non-survivors: median 70 years; Survivors: median 66 years
predictor: Hematocrit (hct) level (low vs. regular vs. high)
notes: 30-day all-cause mortality rate was 44.2%. The proportion of patients with low hct levels was significantly increased in the 30-day mortality group compared to the survival group, while the proportions in the regular and high hct level groups gradually decreased.

source_info.section: **Association of Hematocrit level with 30-day outcomes**

cohort_age: Non-survivors: median 70 years; Survivors: median 66 years
timing: At admission or during ICU stay
notes: Low hct level remained an independent prognostic factor for sepsis after adjustment for confounding factors including age, albumin, platelets, bilirubin, red blood cell distribution width, lactate, Scr, SBP, DBP, MAP, SAPS II score, hypertension, heart failure, and mechanical ventilation.

source_info.section: **Kaplan-Meier survival curve analysis**

population: Patients with sepsis admitted to ICU
cohort_age: Non-survivors: median 70 years; Survivors: median 66 years
method: ROC analysis, multivariable logistic regression, restricted cubic spline
effect_size: Optimal cutoff: HCT 30.15%; OR for low HCT (<30.15%) vs normal: Not reported separately; HCT treated as continuous and categorical variable
notes: U-shaped association between HCT and 30-day mortality observed via restricted cubic spline analysis; both low and high HCT associated with increased mortality
